title: Spread & Edge
subtitle: Width, compensation, and when to widen
intro: The spread is the distance between your bid and ask: spread = ask − bid. It is the market maker's primary source of gross edge on a round-trip — buy at the bid, sell at the ask, keep the difference.

A tight spread attracts flow but leaves little cushion if you are wrong about fair value or if the product moves against your inventory. A wide spread protects you but may see less trading. Interview games reward quotes that are tight enough to trade yet wide enough to survive adverse selection.

Edge is not guaranteed profit on every fill. You earn the spread on average when you are centered on fair value and manage inventory. When customers know more than you, they pick off the wrong side of your quote — that is adverse selection, and the spread is partly payment for that risk.
This lesson trains you to compute spreads, reason about width, and connect spread choice to uncertainty.

Larry Harris emphasizes that the spread compensates market makers for three costs: order-processing, inventory holding, and adverse selection. In interview dice games you feel all three at once: fills arrive fast, position swings quickly, and sharper classmates trade when your quote is stale.

Calibration matters as much as width. A spread that looks tight on paper but sits far from fair value gets picked off; a wide spread centered on fair may earn less gross edge per trade but survive longer. Practice judging spread in units of uncertainty — widen when variance or information risk rises.

questions.question: You quote 38 at 42. What is your spread?

explanations.A: 2 would be half the actual gap or a miscalculation.
explanations.C: 6 is too wide for this quote.
explanations.D: 80 is the sum, not the spread.

remediation.question: Quote 55 at 58. Spread =
Bid 100, ask 105. Spread width:

questions.question: You quote 10 at 14. The midpoint of your quote is:

explanations.A: 10 is the bid, not the center.

remediation.question: 20 at 30. Midpoint:
Symmetric quote around fair value 50 with spread 4 should be:

questions.question: Fair value is 60 but you are very uncertain. Which quote is most appropriate?

explanations.A: Spread of 2 is tight for high uncertainty — little protection.
explanations.B: Spread of 4 is better but may still be tight if volatility is high.
explanations.C: Correct. Wider spread (10) compensates for uncertainty while still roughly centered on 60.
explanations.D: Zero spread offers no edge and is unrealistic.

remediation.question: More uncertainty generally means you should quote a:
Fair 100, spread 8, centered. Best quote:

questions.question: Spread is 3. You complete one round-trip (buy at bid, sell at ask) of size 1. Gross edge before costs is:

explanations.B: 1.5 would be half the spread — that's per-side edge if you only count one leg incorrectly.
explanations.C: Correct. Full round-trip captures the entire spread of 3.

remediation.question: Spread 5, one round-trip, size 2 units. Gross edge:
If spread is 0, round-trip gross edge is:

questions.question: A informed trader always buys when your ask is below true fair value. This hurts you because of:

explanations.A: Inventory matters but the scenario describes being picked off on price.
explanations.B: Correct. They trade when your quote is wrong — you sell too cheap.
explanations.C: Wide spread would protect, not cause, the problem.
explanations.D: Round-trip helps when centered; one-sided informed flow does not.

remediation.question: Spread partly compensates for:
options: Being picked off by better-informed traders

explanations.D: You quote bid and ask, not only mid.

remediation.question: If everyone trading with you is uninformed, you generally can quote:

explanations.B: Correct — less adverse selection risk allows tighter quotes.

questions.question: Fair value jumps to 70. Your old quote was 65 at 67. Best immediate action:
options: Re-center near 70 with a reasonable spread

explanations.A: Stale quote — you'd sell far below fair at 67.
explanations.B: Correct. Update to reflect new fair (e.g. 68 at 72) and avoid being picked off.
explanations.D: You may widen or pause briefly, but the skill is updating, not quitting.

remediation.question: A crossed quote means:

explanations.C: Crossed is bid ≥ ask, zero spread is bid = ask.

remediation.question: When fair value moves, a good market maker:

questions.question: You quote 44 at 48 on a fair-7 dice game. You buy 1 at 44, then later sell 1 at 48 with no fair-value change. What is your gross P&L on the round-trip?

explanations.A: Correct. Buy at 44, sell at 48 → profit 4, equal to the spread width.
explanations.B: 2 would be half the spread — that is per-side edge in some models, not full round-trip.
explanations.C: Zero ignores the captured spread when fair value is unchanged.
explanations.D: −4 would mean you lost the spread; you earned it when centered and flat.

remediation.question: Buy at 30, sell at 35, no other costs. Gross profit:

explanations.B: 2.5 is half the gap, not the round-trip.
explanations.D: You bought low and sold high.

remediation.question: Spread is 6. One full round-trip (buy bid, sell ask) earns:

explanations.B: 3 might be half-spread per leg, not total.
explanations.D: Zero ignores edge when fair is stable.

questions.question: Fair value on a product is 50, but a hidden card will be revealed in 10 seconds and could move fair by ±15. You are currently 49 at 51. What is the most defensible adjustment?
options: Widen materially (e.g., 45 at 55) while re-centering on 50
Tighten to 49.5 at 50.5 to attract flow before the reveal

explanations.A: Correct. Large imminent information risk is classic adverse-selection territory; Harris would widen to cover pick-off risk while staying near fair.
explanations.B: Tightening before a volatility event invites informed traders to hit the wrong side.
explanations.C: A 2-point spread is tiny relative to ±15 fair-value swing — you would be picked off.
explanations.D: One-sided quoting may be allowed at limits, but here the priority is widening two-sided protection.

remediation.question: Variance of payoff just doubled. Spread should generally:

explanations.A: Correct — more risk warrants more compensation.

remediation.question: You are very confident fair = 100 with tiny error. Spread should be:

explanations.A: Correct — low uncertainty allows tighter quotes.
explanations.D: Spread is always defined in practice.

questions.question: Fair is 20. You quote 18 at 22 (spread 4). A buyer lifts you once at the ask, fair unchanged. Relative to fair, how many points of edge did you earn on that single sell?

explanations.A: Correct. Ask 22 is 2 above fair 20 — you capture half the spread on a one-sided sell.
explanations.B: 4 is the full spread; you only sold once, not round-tripped.
explanations.C: 1 would undercount the distance from fair to ask.
explanations.D: Zero ignores that 22 > 20 when fair is unchanged.

remediation.question: Fair 10, quote 8 at 12. You buy at bid. Edge vs fair:

explanations.C: There is edge vs fair on the buy.

remediation.question: Fair 50, quote 48 at 52. Midpoint of quote is:

questions.question: Two candidates quote the same fair-100 product: Alice 97 at 103 (spread 6), Bob 99 at 101 (spread 2). Fair is stable. Who earns more gross edge per completed round-trip, and who is more exposed to a ±5 fair-value shock on a one-sided fill?
options: Alice earns more per round-trip; Bob is more exposed on one-sided fills
Bob earns more per round-trip; Alice is more exposed
Bob earns more and is less exposed

explanations.A: Correct. Alice captures 6 per round-trip vs Bob's 2, but Bob's tight quote leaves only 1 point cushion vs fair on each side — a 5-point move hurts more relative to earned edge.
explanations.B: Reverses both comparisons — Bob's spread is narrower, not wider.
explanations.C: Spread width directly differs on edge and cushion.
explanations.D: Bob earns less per trip and has thinner protection.

remediation.question: Spread 8 vs spread 2 on same fair. Round-trip edge is higher for:

explanations.A: Correct — wider spread, more round-trip edge.
explanations.D: Fair is the same; compare spreads.

remediation.question: Tighter spread generally means:
options: Less edge per trade, more pick-off sensitivity

placementQuestions.question: If fair value rises, a market maker typically:
Edge on a round-trip comes from crossing:
options: Spread once buy and once sell

placementQuestions.question: Tighter spread when you are very confident because:
options: Less need to charge for uncertainty
